publications.editedComment: Three labels ride into this form: Tier, Group, Difficulty. Tier can come from TIER_PRESETS or a custom field capped at one hundred characters. Group takes two hundred and warns that matching strings link scenarios together. Difficulty runs from one Easy to ten Hard, displays unset when null, and has a Clear button that puts it back there. Every choice writes straight into scenarioStore.scenarioForm. Not much of a shootout, listener, but at least every bullet lands in the field it was aimed at.

The classification bath has three temperatures. Tier offers familiar presets but also accepts a custom name. Group binds its text directly to the scenario form and quietly explains that matching strings create a relationship. Difficulty rests on a one-to-ten range, showing Easy, Medium, and Hard as landmarks; Clear returns it to null rather than pretending five was chosen. All three controls share the same computed scenarioStore.scenarioForm. A small room, perhaps, but each guest may choose the water without asking an attendant to carry the answer elsewhere.

Five introductions are permitted because five is where the corridor turns back into itself. Existing intros may arrive as an array or as one string divided by three dashes; either way, the component presents at least one textarea and never more than five. Each entry can be edited, removed, or sent to builder.callSuggest for refinement. Generate all is available only when title or description contains something real, then parses dashed or numbered suggestions back into the sheet. Errors are displayed below. This is not madness. Losing the sixth intro before it exists is simply good architecture.

One intro remains when all others are gone. No more than five are allowed. Add. Remove. Edit. Refine. The sheet accepts arrays, or old strings separated by three dashes. Generate all waits for a title or description, then calls the same suggestion path and cuts the result back to five entries. While suggesting or saving, the buttons wait. Errors stay visible. A story needs an entrance. It does not need six. Sit. Choose the first line carefully.

This gallery keeps its relationships in presentable order. Connected, Available, and All modes compare each scenario’s Dream ids against an explicit context Dream or the workspace selection. Without the required Dream, it offers a Dream gallery instead of displaying an empty casket. Public, owner-only, and mature visibility are filtered before search examines titles, descriptions, locations, genres, inspirations, Dreams, and Characters. Add initializes a clean form tied to the Dream; Clone removes the id, prefixes Copy of, and makes the result private. Selection opens the scenario in workspace and emits the original row. Dignity, even for duplicates.

I like knowing which stories are sitting together. This gallery resolves its Dream from the prop or workspace, then lets Connected, Available, or All decide which ScenarioCards enter the room. Guests see public scenarios or their own; mature rows remain outside unless invited. Search listens across scenario text, related Dreams, and Character names, roles, classes, species, and genres. Add carries the current Dream into a fresh form. Clone creates a private Copy of the original. Selecting a card opens that scenario with the same Dream context. No vaporizing relationships between tables; I keep every chair’s place.

The canvas drifts rather than falls. Twenty to forty-four lanterns rise through four tide bands, while reduced motion keeps twelve lanterns and two bands singing more slowly. Pointer movement wakes a small ripple no more often than every hundred fifteen milliseconds; a press makes a stronger ring. Nearby lanterns curve around the pointer, then wrap beyond the canvas margins instead of disappearing. ResizeObserver preserves their relative positions, and the device-pixel ratio is capped at two. When the component leaves, its animation frame, observer, motion listener, and pointer listeners are released... mm, the long note is allowed to end.

reviewer.name: The Leviathan That Knows Your Name

publications.editedComment: The lanterns remember the dimensions of their water. Resize the canvas and their positions scale before the population is reseeded. The count remains between twenty and forty-four, unless reduced motion calls only twelve from the dark. Pointer movement adds a faint ring at a bounded interval; pointerdown adds a stronger one, and no more than ten ripples may remain. Lanterns within two hundred twenty pixels bend around the touch, then drift upward and wrap beyond the margins. Four tide bands become two when stillness is requested. The frame is cancelled and every listener is removed. You will not stay forever. The canvas already knows.

When no screen effects remain, this control leaves the stage entirely. When one or more are active, Teleport places a fixed button in the body, Transition brings it gently upward, and the badge shows the exact screenEffectCount. Pressing it calls animationStore.clearScreenEffects once. Its right and bottom offsets honor safe-area insets, and the smaller-screen rule reduces the footprint without hiding the words clear all. A close icon, a count, a single exit... then half-light. The next effect may wait until the audience has breathed.

GNASH sees every effect piled up and gets one button, yeah? The button only appears when screenEffectCount is above zero. It teleports to the body, stays fixed over the show at z-index 9999, and wears the exact count in a badge so nobody pretends the pile is smaller. Hit clear all and animationStore.clearScreenEffects knocks the whole stack down at once. Safe-area offsets keep it out of the corner rubble, and the mobile rule makes it tighter. One press. Everything tall comes down. GNASH approves.

I am not falling. Not yet. Thirty to seventy-two seeds orbit the canvas center, their velocity pulled inward and turned sideways by a small sinusoidal swirl. Reduced motion holds only eighteen seeds and three vine bands instead of six. A pointer press creates one pulse and pushes every seed outward according to distance; only six pulses may remain. ResizeObserver scales each existing position, while the pixel ratio stops at two. The seeds cross a margin and re-enter from the opposite edge. On unmount the frame stops, the observer disconnects, and the motion listener releases. The pointer itself passes through the canvas. Four seconds held... and still no wire.

The court has heard the matter of motion. Each seed is governed by a modest pull toward the canvas center, a rotating influence, damping, and an old boundary law: cross the thirty-pixel margin and return from the opposite side. A pointerdown pulse repels the assembly by distance, and precedent limits the record to six fading rings. The garden ordinarily keeps thirty to seventy-two seeds and six vine bands; reduced-motion petitioners receive eighteen seeds and three bands. ResizeObserver scales existing positions, device-pixel ratio is capped at two, and departure cancels the frame and listeners. Balance, petitioner, is not stillness. It is law applied every frame.
